items.q: What colour is the earth wire in a UK three-pin plug?
items.why: Students mix up the wires: live is brown, neutral is blue, and earth is green-and-yellow striped.

items.q: What is the job of a fuse in a plug?
items.correct: To melt and break the circuit if the current gets too high
items.random_wrong: To store charge like a battery
items.why: Students think a fuse boosts current; really it is a deliberate weak point that breaks the circuit to prevent overheating.

items.q: Electrical power is calculated using which equation?
items.correct: P = V x I (power = voltage x current)
items.why: Students rearrange the formula wrongly; power equals voltage multiplied by current, so a higher-power appliance draws more current at mains voltage.

items.q: What colour is the earth wire in a UK plug?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Green and yellow. “Brown” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: UK mains electricity is supplied at…
items.why: It's 230 V, 50 Hz AC. “12 V DC” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the frequency of UK mains electricity, in Hz?
items.why: Exact answer: 50. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
